prompt: Is there a general method for solving a first-price sealed-bid auction with two bidders who have independent private values drawn from different distributions (i.e., ex-ante asymmetric bidders)?
article:
Yes. Under the usual regularity conditions, the **general method** is:

1) **restrict attention to strictly increasing (monotone) pure strategies** so bids are invertible,  
2) write each bidder’s **first‐order optimality condition** (FOC) against the opponent’s inverse bid function,  
3) obtain a **coupled system of ODEs in the inverse bid functions** with **boundary conditions at the bottom and top of the bid/value supports**, and  
4) solve that **two‑point / free‑boundary value problem** (typically numerically; closed forms exist only for special distribution families).

Below is the standard characterization, what assumptions make it valid, and how one actually solves it in practice.

---

## 1) Baseline model and what “solving” means

- Two bidders \(i\in\{1,2\}\).
- Independent private values \(V_i\sim F_i\) with (typically) continuous densities \(f_i\) on an interval support. Bidders may be **ex‑ante asymmetric**: \(F_1\neq F_2\).
- Risk neutral payoff if bidder \(i\) bids \(b\) and wins: \(u_i = V_i - b\); if loses: \(0\).
- First‑price sealed bid: highest bid wins and pays own bid. With continuous types and strictly increasing strategies, ties occur with probability 0, so tie‑breaking is usually irrelevant for the ODE characterization (but it matters in discrete/atomic cases) [[1]](https://maskin.scholars.harvard.edu/sites/g/files/omnuum10606/files/maskin/files/equilibrium_in_sealed_high_bid_auctions.pdf).

A Bayes–Nash equilibrium in (strictly) increasing pure strategies is a pair of bid functions
\[
\beta_i:\text{values}\to \text{bids},\qquad \beta_i' > 0,
\]
and solving the auction means finding \(\beta_1,\beta_2\).

---

## 2) The key reduction: invertibility and the best‑response problem

Assume \(\beta_j\) is strictly increasing, so it has an inverse
\[
\alpha_j(b) := \beta_j^{-1}(b).
\]

If bidder \(i\) with value \(v\) submits bid \(b\), he wins iff \(b \ge \beta_j(V_j)\), i.e. iff \(V_j \le \alpha_j(b)\). Independence implies the win probability is
\[
\Pr(\text{win}\mid b) = F_j(\alpha_j(b)).
\]

So bidder \(i\)’s expected payoff from bidding \(b\) is
\[
\pi_i(b;v) = (v-b)\,F_j(\alpha_j(b)).
\]

In equilibrium, bidder \(i\) with value \(v\) chooses \(b=\beta_i(v)\). Equivalently, if we evaluate things at a bid \(b\) that is actually used by bidder \(i\), then \(v=\alpha_i(b)\).

---

## 3) Standard equilibrium characterization: coupled ODEs (two bidders)

### 3.1 FOC in a convenient form

Different authors write the equilibrium conditions in slightly different but equivalent ways. A very common and useful form is the **log‑derivative condition** (Lebrun’s characterization) [[4]](https://econ.laps.yorku.ca/files/2015/10/lebrunb-u.pdf):

For two bidders, on the interior of the winning‑bid support,
\[
\frac{d}{db}\ln F_1(\alpha_1(b)) \;=\; \frac{1}{\alpha_2(b)-b},
\qquad
\frac{d}{db}\ln F_2(\alpha_2(b)) \;=\; \frac{1}{\alpha_1(b)-b}.
\tag{ODE-L}
\]
This is the \(n=2\) specialization of Lebrun’s general \(n\)-bidder ODE system and is obtained directly from each bidder’s FOC [[4]](https://econ.laps.yorku.ca/files/2015/10/lebrunb-u.pdf).

An equivalent (often more “computational”) form comes from differentiating \(F_j(\alpha_j(b))\):
\[
(\alpha_i(b)-b)\, f_j(\alpha_j(b))\,\alpha_j'(b) = F_j(\alpha_j(b)),
\]
so
\[
\alpha_1'(b)=\frac{F_1(\alpha_1(b))}{f_1(\alpha_1(b))(\alpha_2(b)-b)},
\qquad
\alpha_2'(b)=\frac{F_2(\alpha_2(b))}{f_2(\alpha_2(b))(\alpha_1(b)-b)}.
\tag{ODE-α}
\]
This is just (ODE‑L) rewritten.

Once \(\alpha_1,\alpha_2\) are found, you recover the bid functions by inversion:
\[
\beta_i(v) = \alpha_i^{-1}(v).
\]

### 3.2 Boundary conditions (why it’s a boundary/free‑boundary problem)

You need boundary conditions at **both ends**:

- **Top boundary (common maximal bid):** under common upper support \( \bar v\) and standard regularity, it is a key property used throughout the literature that the highest valuation types submit the **same maximal equilibrium bid** (a “common top bid”), giving
\[
\alpha_1(\bar b)=\bar v,\qquad \alpha_2(\bar b)=\bar v,
\]
with \(\bar b\) unknown a priori (a “free boundary”) [[6]](https://brocku.ca/repec/pdf/0504.pdf), [[18]](http://www.math.tau.ac.il/~fibich/Manuscripts/Asymmetric-first-price-auctions-boundary-layer.pdf). Lebrun expresses this as \(\alpha_1(\eta)=\alpha_2(\eta)=d\) in common‑support notation [[4]](https://econ.laps.yorku.ca/files/2015/10/lebrunb-u.pdf).

- **Bottom boundary (behavior at the lower endpoint):** in the atomless common‑support case with nonbinding reserve, equilibrium has \(\beta_i(\underline v)=\underline v\) (often normalized to 0), so
\[
\alpha_1(\underline b)=\underline v,\qquad \alpha_2(\underline b)=\underline v.
\]
Lebrun’s characterization makes clear that the exact lower boundary condition depends on whether there are **atoms at the lower endpoint** (or an effective atom induced by a reserve price). In particular, a bidder can have \(\alpha_i(\underline b)>\underline v\) (flat bidding at the bottom) only if the *other* bidder’s distribution has mass at the lower endpoint [[4]](https://econ.laps.yorku.ca/files/2015/10/lebrunb-u.pdf). This matters for uniqueness/existence.

**Critical practical point:** the ODEs are typically **singular at the lower endpoint** (terms behave like \(0/0\) or violate Lipschitz conditions), which is why solving from the bottom as an initial value problem is numerically delicate [[19]](https://capcp.la.psu.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/11/2020/07/NumericalSolutions.pdf), [[18]](http://www.math.tau.ac.il/~fibich/Manuscripts/Asymmetric-first-price-auctions-boundary-layer.pdf), [[7]](https://vinci.cs.uiowa.edu/~hjp/download/hkp.pdf).

---

## 4) Existence/monotonicity and uniqueness: when this ODE method is valid

### 4.1 Monotone pure strategies: why you can assume invertibility

- Maskin–Riley provide conditions (independence plus preference restrictions like single‑crossing / supermodularity‑type conditions) ensuring **monotonicity of best responses** and underpinning monotone equilibrium analysis in sealed high‑bid auctions [[1]](https://maskin.scholars.harvard.edu/sites/g/files/omnuum10606/files/maskin/files/equilibrium_in_sealed_high_bid_auctions.pdf).  
- In the standard risk‑neutral IPV model with continuous distributions, classic results (Lebrun; Maskin–Riley) establish existence of equilibrium with **continuous, strictly increasing** bidding strategies under common assumptions (atomless, common support, positive densities on interior, etc.) [[5]](https://kylewoodward.com/blog-data/pdfs/references/kirkegaard-journal-of-economic-theory-2009A.pdf).

So the ODE characterization is the “standard” route precisely because monotonicity implies invertibility.

### 4.2 Uniqueness is subtle and depends on boundary behavior

Lebrun’s analysis highlights that uniqueness is driven by how the boundary conditions pin down the solution of the ODE system:

- In the common‑support case, **if there are mass points at the lower endpoint** (or a binding reserve that creates them), uniqueness is obtained [[4]](https://econ.laps.yorku.ca/files/2015/10/lebrunb-u.pdf).
- If a **reserve price is binding**, you can “collapse” probability below the reserve into an atom at the reserve, effectively producing a mass point at the new lower boundary; Lebrun notes this yields uniqueness even when the original distributions were atomless [[4]](https://econ.laps.yorku.ca/files/2015/10/lebrunb-u.pdf).
- Without such mass points and with atomless distributions, uniqueness is harder; Lebrun provides additional sufficient conditions (e.g., distinct lower endpoints or local strict log‑concavity / decreasing reverse hazard rates near the highest lower endpoint) ensuring uniqueness in broader asymmetric‑support settings [[4]](https://econ.laps.yorku.ca/files/2015/10/lebrunb-u.pdf).

Maskin–Riley’s uniqueness work similarly leans on having **positive mass at the lower endpoint**, noting that even a slight reserve price above the lowest possible value can ensure this [[8]](https://www.ias.edu/sites/default/files/sss/papers/econpaper31.pdf). They also stress that to make uniqueness meaningful one commonly restricts attention to equilibria where bidders do **not bid above value/reservation price** [[8]](https://www.ias.edu/sites/default/files/sss/papers/econpaper31.pdf).

### 4.3 Multiple equilibria if you allow “off‑path” overbidding (important caveat)

A major caveat: uniqueness results such as Maskin–Riley and Lebrun are typically for equilibria satisfying a **no‑overbidding restriction** (bid \(\le\) value). Kaplan–Zamir show that if you relax this restriction, asymmetric first‑price auctions can have **additional (non‑standard) equilibria** in which some bidders sometimes bid above value but **never win when doing so**, yet revenue and allocation can differ across equilibria [[12]](https://mpra.ub.uni-muenchen.de/34937/1/MPRA_paper_34937.pdf). So the “general method” usually targets the standard monotone/no‑overbidding equilibrium.

### 4.4 Tie‑breaking and existence pathologies (usually not an issue in smooth IPV, but relevant with atoms/discrete types)

Maskin–Riley emphasize that the sealed high‑bid auction is discontinuous and that under standard random tie‑breaking, best responses (and hence equilibria) can fail to exist in some environments; they discuss alternative tie‑breaking rules that restore existence in certain examples [[1]](https://maskin.scholars.harvard.edu/sites/g/files/omnuum10606/files/maskin/files/equilibrium_in_sealed_high_bid_auctions.pdf). In continuous atomless two‑bidder IPV settings, ties are probability‑zero, so this rarely affects the ODE approach; with atoms/reserves it can matter for boundary behavior.

---

## 5) How you actually “solve” the asymmetric two‑bidder FPA

### 5.1 Conceptually: it’s a coupled ODE boundary/free‑boundary problem

You solve (ODE‑L) or (ODE‑α) subject to boundary conditions at bottom and top, with an unknown maximal bid \(\bar b\). Lebrun’s proof strategy (and many numerical methods) can be viewed as:

- Treat the top boundary as the “initial condition” (since it is well behaved),
- integrate **backwards** in \(b\),
- and choose \(\bar b\) so that the solution hits the correct lower boundary condition [[4]](https://econ.laps.yorku.ca/files/2015/10/lebrunb-u.pdf), [[19]](https://capcp.la.psu.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/11/2020/07/NumericalSolutions.pdf).

This is why you’ll often see the asymmetric FPA described as a two‑point boundary value problem with a free boundary (the maximal bid).

### 5.2 Why closed forms are rare; known analytic special cases

In general, the coupled ODE system does not yield closed forms. Analytic solutions exist mainly for special distribution families:

- **Uniform values with different supports:** Kaplan–Zamir solve the general two‑uniform‑support case (including different lower endpoints) and also analyze a binding minimum bid; they derive explicit formulas by reducing the inverse‑bid ODE system to a single ODE using algebraic identities and boundary conditions [[26]](http://www.ma.huji.ac.il/~zamir/documents/Uniform_fulltext.pdf), [[30]](https://ratio.huji.ac.il/files/dp432.pdf). (They also show linear bidding occurs in specific parameter regions.)  
- Earlier restricted uniform cases trace to **Griesmer–Levitan–Shubik (1967)** (as described by Kaplan–Zamir) [[26]](http://www.ma.huji.ac.il/~zamir/documents/Uniform_fulltext.pdf), [[33]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/wly/navlog/v14y1967i4p415-433.html).  
- **Plum (1992)** characterizes equilibria via a singular ODE boundary value problem for a broader class of “\(\lambda\)-auctions” (including first‑price) and reports explicit solvability for certain asymmetric classes (including uniform) [[31]](https://kylewoodward.com/blog-data/pdfs/references/plum-international-journal-of-game-theory-1992A.pdf).  
- Kaplan–Zamir also summarize analytic “power distribution” cases (Plum; Cheng) but under support/parameter restrictions [[26]](http://www.ma.huji.ac.il/~zamir/documents/Uniform_fulltext.pdf).

These explicit solutions are valuable as benchmarks for numerical solvers, but they are exceptions.

---

## 6) Numerical methods in practice (the “general method” operationalized)

Because closed forms are rare, “solving” typically means numerically computing \(\alpha_1,\alpha_2\) (or \(\beta_1,\beta_2\)).

### 6.1 The legacy standard: backward shooting (but can be unstable)

Historically, the “standard method” is **backward shooting**: guess \(\bar b\), impose \(\alpha_1(\bar b)=\alpha_2(\bar b)=\bar v\), integrate the ODEs backward, and adjust \(\bar b\) until the lower boundary condition is met [[7]](https://vinci.cs.uiowa.edu/~hjp/download/hkp.pdf), [[19]](https://capcp.la.psu.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/11/2020/07/NumericalSolutions.pdf).  

However, Fibich–Gavish show backward shooting is **inherently unstable** for asymmetric first‑price auctions (instability worsens with more bidders) [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0899825611000509). This is why modern implementations often prefer alternative boundary‑value or fixed‑point approaches.

### 6.2 Boundary‑value / fixed‑point / Newton approaches (stable alternatives)

Fibich–Gavish propose a **boundary‑value method** as a robust alternative to backward shooting and demonstrate stability across bidder counts and distribution types (including multiple crossings) [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0899825611000509). Gavish provides Matlab implementations using **fixed‑point iterations** and **Newton iterations**, including two‑player code that can also compute revenue [[22]](https://ngavish.net.technion.ac.il/matlab-code-for-asymmetric-first-price-auctions/).

### 6.3 Local series expansion / high‑accuracy stepping

Gayle–Richard provide an automated algorithm based on **recursive local Taylor series expansions** over a grid, designed for accuracy and robustness; it still uses backward extrapolation because the system is ill‑defined at the origin and iterates to find the unknown endpoint [[19]](https://capcp.la.psu.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/11/2020/07/NumericalSolutions.pdf). They emphasize the method can compute not only bids but also expected revenue, win probabilities, retention probabilities under reserve prices, and optimal reserves via quadrature [[19]](https://capcp.la.psu.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/11/2020/07/NumericalSolutions.pdf).

### 6.4 Polynomial / spectral approximation + constrained residual minimization (MPEC flavor)

Another family of methods approximates inverse bid functions by polynomials (e.g., Chebyshev basis) and chooses coefficients to minimize ODE residuals on a grid subject to boundary and monotonicity constraints—often cast as a constrained optimization / MPEC‑type problem [[7]](https://vinci.cs.uiowa.edu/~hjp/download/hkp.pdf). Hubbard–Kirkegaard–Paarsch emphasize this can be faster than shooting (important for estimation loops), but quality depends on a good initial guess and sufficient polynomial degree; low‑degree approximations can produce misleading counterfactual revenue rankings [[7]](https://vinci.cs.uiowa.edu/~hjp/download/hkp.pdf).

### 6.5 Discretize actions and use indifference rather than ODEs

Au–Banks–Guo propose the **Backward Indifference Derivation (BID)** algorithm: approximate the continuum auction with a sequence of finite‑action games whose PSNE converge to the continuum equilibrium, computing where bidders are indifferent between adjacent actions rather than directly integrating “poorly behaved” ODEs [[23]](https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/10.1287/deca.2021.0432). Their convergence guarantees are conditional on knowing the maximum bid [[23]](https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/10.1287/deca.2021.0432)—again highlighting the centrality of the free boundary.

### 6.6 Learning / neural approximations (post‑2020 direction)

Recent work explores learning equilibrium strategies via gradient‑based self‑play with function approximators:

- Bichler–Kohring–Heidekrüger extend neural pseudogradient ascent to asymmetric auction models and report close approximation to known BNE benchmarks, enabling analysis in larger environments without analytic solutions [[24]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/inm/orijoc/v35y2023i3p523-542.html).  
- A later theoretical line (Bichler et al.) frames equilibrium as an infinite‑dimensional variational inequality and studies convergence/uniqueness properties relevant to learning‑based solvers [[25]](https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/33491/35646).

These methods are not the classical “general method,” but they are increasingly used when ODE/BVP approaches are hard to stabilize or extend.

---

## 7) Once you have \(\alpha_i\) or \(\beta_i\): computing revenue, winning probabilities, efficiency

A virtue of solving for **inverse bids** is that many objects become straightforward integrals.

### 7.1 Expected revenue

For first price, revenue is the expected winning bid:
\[
R = \mathbb{E}\big[\max\{\beta_1(V_1),\beta_2(V_2)\}\big].
\]
Fibich–Gavious provide an equivalent integral expression in terms of inverse bids and the maximal bid \(\bar b\) (for \(n\) bidders; specialize to 2 bidders by taking the product over \(k=1,2\)) [[36]](http://www.math.tau.ac.il/~fibich/Manuscripts/first_rev_final.pdf):
\[
R \;=\; \bar b \;-\;\int_{\underline b}^{\bar b} F_1(\alpha_1(b))\,F_2(\alpha_2(b))\,db.
\tag{Rev}
\]
This is often numerically convenient once \(\alpha_i\) are known.

### 7.2 Winning probabilities and bidder surplus (envelope method)

Let \(q_i(v)\) be bidder \(i\)’s equilibrium win probability at value \(v\). Kirkegaard uses a mechanism‑design/envelope argument to show in first‑price auctions:
\[
EU_i(v)=(v-\beta_i(v))\,q_i(v),\qquad EU_i'(v)=q_i(v),\qquad
EU_i(v)=\int_0^v q_i(x)\,dx,
\]
and hence
\[
\beta_i(v)=v-\frac{\int_0^v q_i(x)\,dx}{q_i(v)}.
\tag{shade}
\]
So if you can compute \(q_i(\cdot)\), you can recover bids (and vice versa) and compute interim surplus directly [[6]](https://brocku.ca/repec/pdf/0504.pdf).

### 7.3 Efficiency / misallocation

In asymmetric first price, the highest value need not win (bid shading differs across bidders), so efficiency comparisons and revenue comparisons between formats are nontrivial—this is exactly why asymmetry breaks the neat symmetric revenue equivalence logic in applications [[19]](https://capcp.la.psu.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/11/2020/07/NumericalSolutions.pdf), and Maskin–Riley show revenue rankings between formats can reverse depending on how asymmetry changes (stochastic dominance vs shifts/stretching) [[35]](http://www.econ.ucla.edu/riley/research/asyRES.PDF).

Computationally, once \(\beta_i\) are known, efficiency is typically computed by integrating/simulating the event \(\beta_1(V_1)>\beta_2(V_2)\) vs \(V_1>V_2\).

---

## 8) Important extensions/edge cases that change the “general method”

### 8.1 Different supports (non‑common support)

Many standard ODE/BVP formulations assume common support \([0,\bar v]\) (or \([c,d]\)) so that both the lower and upper boundary conditions align across bidders [[18]](http://www.math.tau.ac.il/~fibich/Manuscripts/Asymmetric-first-price-auctions-boundary-layer.pdf), [[36]](http://www.math.tau.ac.il/~fibich/Manuscripts/first_rev_final.pdf), [[4]](https://econ.laps.yorku.ca/files/2015/10/lebrunb-u.pdf). If supports differ, you can get regimes where one bidder’s high types essentially face no competition (or the “Getty effect” in some parameter regions of uniform models), and equilibrium can involve **kinks, flat regions, or even mixed strategies** at boundaries; Kaplan–Zamir handle these complications explicitly in the uniform family [[26]](http://www.ma.huji.ac.il/~zamir/documents/Uniform_fulltext.pdf), [[30]](https://ratio.huji.ac.il/files/dp432.pdf). In general distributions, non‑common support changes boundary conditions and sometimes the very structure of the active bidding region; there is no single universal closed‑form recipe beyond “re‑derive the active region and boundary conditions, then solve the corresponding ODE/BVP.”

### 8.2 Reserve prices / minimum bids

Reserve prices often *help* with uniqueness by inducing an effective mass point at the reserve (boundary pinning) [[4]](https://econ.laps.yorku.ca/files/2015/10/lebrunb-u.pdf), [[8]](https://www.ias.edu/sites/default/files/sss/papers/econpaper31.pdf). But they can also create **discontinuities** and **piecewise** equilibrium structures; Kotowski’s analysis of asymmetric reserve prices exhibits semi‑separating vs separating regimes with explicit threshold conditions and sometimes discontinuous bidding by one group at a cutoff value [[44]](https://econtheory.org/ojs/index.php/te/article/viewFile/20180205/19842/602). Numerically, you solve separate ODE pieces with matching conditions at thresholds.

### 8.3 Risk aversion / non‑quasilinear utilities

The ODE approach extends, but the exact functional form changes. Maskin–Riley’s uniqueness analysis is explicitly set up for general \(U_i(b,s_i)\) and uses a single‑crossing condition to obtain monotonicity and derive an ODE system in transformed objects [[8]](https://www.ias.edu/sites/default/files/sss/papers/econpaper31.pdf). (This is one of the cleanest “general method” templates beyond risk neutrality.)

### 8.4 Correlation / general information structures (not IPV)

If values are correlated or bidders observe signals with arbitrary information structures, the classical two‑bidder asymmetric IPV ODE approach is no longer the right general tool. Work like Bergemann–Brooks–Morris instead characterizes bounds on equilibrium outcomes across information structures using deviation‑based programs rather than solving bidder ODEs [[39]](https://cramton.umd.edu/market-design-papers/bergemann-brooks-morris-first-price-auctions-bidding-and-revenue.pdf), [[40]](https://www.benjaminbrooks.net/downloads/bbm_firstpriceauctions.pdf). Computationally, correlated priors can lead to hardness/approximation regimes studied in algorithmic work (e.g., approximate equilibrium computation under correlated priors) [[41]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.05322). Existence theory in first‑price auctions with correlation/atoms has also advanced recently [[42]](https://www.econometricsociety.org/publications/econometrica/2026/01/01/Equilibrium-Existence-in-First-Price-Auctions-with-Private-Values), [[43]](https://www.econometricsociety.org/publications/econometrica/2026/01/01/Equilibrium-Existence-in-First-Price-Auctions-with-Private-Values/file/ecta70003.pdf).

---

## 9) A practical “general method” checklist (two bidders, asymmetric IPV)

Assuming you want the **standard monotone/no‑overbidding equilibrium**:

1) **Normalize supports** if common support is plausible; otherwise identify the region where both bidders can win (may be piecewise).  
2) Assume strictly increasing \(\beta_i\) and work with inverses \(\alpha_i\).  
3) Write the coupled ODE system (ODE‑L) or (ODE‑α) [[4]](https://econ.laps.yorku.ca/files/2015/10/lebrunb-u.pdf).  
4) Impose boundary conditions:
   - \(\alpha_i(\bar b)=\bar v\) (common top bid; \(\bar b\) unknown) [[4]](https://econ.laps.yorku.ca/files/2015/10/lebrunb-u.pdf), [[18]](http://www.math.tau.ac.il/~fibich/Manuscripts/Asymmetric-first-price-auctions-boundary-layer.pdf);  
   - lower boundary depends on atoms/reserve; with atomless common support often \(\alpha_i(\underline b)=\underline v\) [[4]](https://econ.laps.yorku.ca/files/2015/10/lebrunb-u.pdf).  
5) Solve the resulting **free‑boundary BVP**:
   - avoid naive backward shooting if stability is an issue [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0899825611000509);
   - use boundary‑value / fixed‑point / Newton methods [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0899825611000509), [[22]](https://ngavish.net.technion.ac.il/matlab-code-for-asymmetric-first-price-auctions/), or robust stepping/Taylor schemes [[19]](https://capcp.la.psu.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/11/2020/07/NumericalSolutions.pdf), or polynomial/MPEC approximations with monotonicity constraints [[7]](https://vinci.cs.uiowa.edu/~hjp/download/hkp.pdf).  
6) Validate:
   - check monotonicity and FOC residuals;
   - if distributions cross, verify predicted bid crossings (a known diagnostic theme in the numerical literature) [[7]](https://vinci.cs.uiowa.edu/~hjp/download/hkp.pdf).  
7) Compute outcomes (revenue, win probabilities, efficiency) from \(\alpha_i\)/\(\beta_i\) using integrals such as (Rev) [[36]](http://www.math.tau.ac.il/~fibich/Manuscripts/first_rev_final.pdf) and/or simulation; compute utilities via envelope identities if desired [[6]](https://brocku.ca/repec/pdf/0504.pdf).

---

## Bottom line

- **The general analytical method** for two asymmetric IPV bidders in a first‑price sealed‑bid auction is to use **invertibility + first‑order conditions** to derive a **coupled ODE system in inverse bid functions with boundary conditions** (a boundary/free‑boundary value problem) [[4]](https://econ.laps.yorku.ca/files/2015/10/lebrunb-u.pdf).  
- **In general distributions, you should expect to solve it numerically**, because closed forms are exceptional (notably certain uniform/power families) [[26]](http://www.ma.huji.ac.il/~zamir/documents/Uniform_fulltext.pdf), [[31]](https://kylewoodward.com/blog-data/pdfs/references/plum-international-journal-of-game-theory-1992A.pdf).  
- **Existence/uniqueness** depend heavily on boundary behavior (atoms/reserves/log‑concavity assumptions) and on whether you restrict to the standard no‑overbidding equilibrium; relaxing that restriction can introduce additional equilibria under asymmetry [[4]](https://econ.laps.yorku.ca/files/2015/10/lebrunb-u.pdf), [[8]](https://www.ias.edu/sites/default/files/sss/papers/econpaper31.pdf), [[12]](https://mpra.ub.uni-muenchen.de/34937/1/MPRA_paper_34937.pdf).  
- **Practically**, robust boundary‑value / fixed‑point / constrained approximation methods are preferred over naive backward shooting due to instability results [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0899825611000509), [[7]](https://vinci.cs.uiowa.edu/~hjp/download/hkp.pdf), with several well‑documented numerical alternatives available [[19]](https://capcp.la.psu.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/11/2020/07/NumericalSolutions.pdf), [[22]](https://ngavish.net.technion.ac.il/matlab-code-for-asymmetric-first-price-auctions/), [[23]](https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/10.1287/deca.2021.0432).
